item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As a side note centred should be centered.

It's British English, friend.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

My theory on this is based on the mainlist title for this 'Selfish Preservation'.

+ Theory
- Theory

Obviously this is a pun on 'Self preservation', but why the word 'Selfish'? Well my theory is that the man sacrificed his family either literally or metaphorically to grant himself immortality or to save himself from a disease (probably the cancer that he still has even though it can't kill him anymore.)

Pages of notebooks containing diary entries, small watercolour paintings, and poems centred around the themes of rebirth, youth, and impermanence.

As a side note centred should be centered.

A sketch of an idyllic countryside scene, featuring SCP-4617, the woman from the aforementioned photograph, and two young children, standing in front of a cottage. All but SCP-4617 are smiling.

So this guy was obsessed with his own impermanence and mortality so he developed a method to become immortal. Now whether he literally chopped up his family for raw materials.

Multiple infant teeth.

A single human heart, salted.

Or just neglected them until they died of unrelated causes the result was that he was left with nothing except immortality. I do have a feeling he killed his family based on the 'used matches' though obviously I could be way off.

Anyway, that's just a theory, a SCP theory thanks for reading.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'M DIGGING UP YOUR COFFIN
AND POURING OUT THE CONTENTS
YOUR SEXY SWEET SOLUTION
IS RIGHT FOR DISTRIBUTION
SWEET BOD
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Spoilers kinda?
- Yeah, this is a bit of a spoiler I guess.

Like the author post indicates, this is really a more impressionistic piece than something needing to be figured out, if that makes sense. There are clues hidden in the article that let you kind of piece together the general gist of a story, but nothing is really in your face about it.

Seeing him vomit up the jars with stuff in them is really what clues us in to what's going on in the background. You see a dog collar, vacation photos with a girl, artwork, his wife and kids in a country house, the locket, etc. All indications of a time when stuff was going great for this dude. Then you get to the darker stuff, like the chemicals, motor oil, machinery diagrams, the burned letter, matches, and the preserved human heart. Stuff was great, and then it wasn't so great, and this guy likely lost everything he had. As such, it's heavily implied that he did this to himself in an attempt to get those times back in some way (at least, that was the message I got from it, as well as what Malice hinted at when I was giving him crit).

Hope that helped explain this a bit!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is one of those moments when I am reminded that some forms of human art are readily assimilated by most people, but not me.

If anyone would be willing to provide a collapsable explaining this, I would be most appreciative.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
(p)reserved

I feel dumb. Literally took until reading this author post reservation to get the theme of the article. I already upvoted, but that upvote has now doubled in solidity. Nice job, dude.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Once every 18 days (±15 hours), SCP-4617 will undergo a ζ event, during which time it will violently convulse, tense all muscles, and slowly expel from its mouth a single mason jar (diameter 20 cm) filled with chemically pure water. In approximately 90% of cases, this jar will also contain a single object or document related to SCP-4617 in some way.

Jesus Christ this line still gets me every time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks to Mortos, Weryllium, TheMightyMcB and Vezaz for helping critique this in chat! It's probably the weirdest thing I've done in quite a while, and I'm pleased with how it came out, even if it is a little more impressionistic than my usual stuff.

Image source: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Diesel_in_mason_jar.JPG, in the public domain.
